invariant_definition: Release from bondage, but not a bare escape from existence. *Mokṣa* names the final state appropriate to what a school takes bondage to be: the nexus of *karma*, rebirth, embodiment, ignorance, misidentification, or estrangement from the Lord. It is therefore defined against adjacent terms such as *saṃsāra*, *bandha*, *apavarga*, and *kaivalya*. In the Mīmāṃsā background, the point is that no finite ritual act can simply manufacture *mokṣa* as a produced result; in the Vedāntic setting, the issue becomes what sort of cognition, devotion, surrender, or divine grace removes the condition that makes rebirth possible. The practical register is inseparable from the metaphysics: Advaita ties *mokṣa* to the removal of *avidyā* through *śravaṇa-manana-nididhyāsana*; Viśiṣṭādvaita and Tattva-vāda (Dvaita) tie it to God-directed knowledge and devotion; Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇavism treats even ordinary *mokṣa* as lower than *prema*. What remains invariant is this: *mokṣa* is the non-returning condition in which the structures that sustain saṃsāric agency, enjoyment, and compulsory embodiment no longer bind.

school_framing.shared_core: Every school that retains the word *mokṣa* (or *apavarga*/*kaivalya*/*nirvāṇa*) means **the non-returning condition in which the structures that sustain saṃsāric agency, enjoyment, and compulsory embodiment no longer bind**. The differentia of *mokṣa* is therefore set by what each school takes *bandha* to be: *avidyā* (Advaita), distance from Bhagavān (Viśiṣṭādvaita, Tattva-vāda, Gauḍīya, Śuddhādvaita), *puruṣa-prakṛti-saṃyoga* (Sāṅkhya-Yoga), *duḥkha-pravṛtti-mithyājñāna* (Nyāya), *prapañca-vikalpa* (Mādhyamaka), karmic *pudgala* (Jainism), or contracted *aiśvarya* (Trika).
school_framing.register_axes_note: **Same concept, different aspect** with respect to the structural role 'non-returning end-state'. **Real disagreement** (AF1) between Advaita (*jīva–Brahman* identity in *mokṣa*) and Tattva-vāda (*jīveśvara-bheda* persists, with *taratamya* among the liberated; *Anuvyākhyāna* 2.3.66–69, *Viṣṇu-Tattva-Vinirṇaya* 3.27) — same M-register, same scope-inside, opposing claims. Gauḍīya treats ordinary *mokṣa* as lower than *prema* (*Bhakti-Rasāmṛta-Sindhu* 1.1.11–16): real disagreement about ranking, not about what *mokṣa* itself names.

per_school.definition: *Mokṣa* is *ajñāna-nivṛtti*, not a *kārya* produced by ritual action; since action itself belongs within the economy of ignorance, liberation depends on *jñāna* alone [[UpS I.11.15](cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.11.15); [NS 1.98-99](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.98-99)]. More exactly, *anyadṛṣṭi* is *avidyā*, and the destruction of that misapprehension through the sentence-meaning of the Upaniṣadic *mahāvākya* is what is called *mokṣa* [[UpS I.17.7-9](cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.7-9)]. Accordingly, liberation is present at the very rise of self-knowledge: when the *ātmajñānāgni* burns the seed of saṃsāra without remainder, the knower is already *vimukta* [[IṢ 1.9](cite://vimuktatman/ista-siddhi/1.Jīvanmuktiḥ)].

Vedānta teaches release by making known the *jīva* as distinct from body, the *antaryāmin* Paramātman, the *upāsanā* of him, and the ensuing manifestation of the self together with an unsurpassed experience of Brahmanic bliss [[VS §3](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§3); [VS §4](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§4)]. When *karma*-generated differentiations such as deva-, manuṣya-, and the like are removed, the self's own *svarūpa* remains a *jñānānandaika-guṇa* reality, beyond verbal objectification yet not annihilated [[VS §5](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§5)]. The practical stance toward this release is *prapatti*: the *akiñcana* who is *ananyagati* takes refuge at the Lord's feet as the sole shore beyond *bhavārṇava* [[YStr 22-24](cite://yamuna/stotra-ratna/22-24)].

*Mokṣa* is not yielded by knowledge as an autonomous natural power; Viṣṇu himself gives knowledge to the ignorant, liberation to the knowers, and bliss to the liberated [[Anuvy. 1.1.13-15](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.1.13-15)]. The *jīva* never becomes essentially identical with Īśvara: even in liberation its consciousness and bliss remain as qualities similar to the Lord's, while *jīveśvara-bheda* stands firm by the force of śruti [[Anuvy. 2.3.66-69](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69)]. The soteriological sequence is *vairāgya* -> *bhakti-dārḍhya* -> *upāsanā* -> *Viṣṇv-aparokṣya*, so liberation is inseparable from contemplative devotion [[Anuvy. 3.3.1](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/3.3.1)]. Among the liberated there is enduring *taratamya*: those fit for release rejoice in graded degrees of excellence rather than in an undifferentiated sameness [[VTV 3.27](cite://madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.27)].
per_school.register_tag: M+S; Tattvavādin [REAL-DISAGREEMENT with Advaita on jīveśvara-identity]

per_school.definition: Because the *jīva* is related to Bhagavān by simultaneous *bheda* and *abheda* under his *acintya-śakti*, the *abheda-śāstras* are to be coordinated only after contradiction has been removed by affirming both difference from and non-difference from him [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. Once Bhagavān and the *māyā-mohita* jīva stand thus distinguished, his *bhajana* alone has the status of *abhidheya*, and *parama-prema* for Bhagavān is fixed as the *prayojana* [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §44](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§44); [§45](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§45)]. Accordingly, even *sālokya*, *sārṣṭi*, *sāmīpya*, *sārūpya*, and *ekatva* are refused apart from *mat-sevana*, while pure *uttamā-bhakti* is unoverlaid by *jñāna* and *karma* [[BRS 1.1.11](cite://rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1.11); [BRS 1.1.14](cite://rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1.14); [BRS 1.1.16](cite://rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1.16)].

The jīva is *jñāna-svarūpa*, atomic, a knower, and dependent on Hari, being fit for both conjunction with and disjunction from embodiment [[DaŚ 1](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/1)]. Its bondage is beginningless conjunction with *māyā*, whereas its released condition is known by Bhagavān's grace; the same self is spoken of as bound, liberated, and devoted according to its condition [[DaŚ 2](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/2)]. Hence the jīva is neither absolutely different from nor absolutely non-different from Śrī Puruṣottama, but an *aṃśa* understood as his dependent *śakti* [[VK 2.3.42](cite://srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42)]. The consummation sought is a *kṛpā-phala* beyond mere release, namely the *bhakti-rasa* of the worshipper in relation to the worshipped Lord [[DaŚ 10](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/10)].

Brahman is the *samavāyi-kāraṇa* of the world as *sat-cit-ānanda*, and the jīva is truly Brahman's *aṃśa* rather than an adventitious superimposition [[BSB 1.1.4](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.4); [BSB 2.3.43-45](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.3.43-45)]. With Brahman-knowledge and the cessation of *avidyā*, fitness for one's own *svarūpa* is accomplished for the attainment of Puruṣottama; when the soul is accepted as his own, *bhakti* functions as the necessary *sahakāri* for that attainment [[BSB 1.1.12-19](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.12-19)].

Sāṅkhya treats liberation as the end for which prakṛti's entire evolution is undertaken with respect to each distinct puruṣa, so that the cosmos is teleologically ordered toward vimokṣa rather than toward a single collective consummation [[SK 56](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/56)]. The decisive means is repeated analysis of the tattvas, which issues in a pure and non-errant discriminative knowledge expressed as the total withdrawal of self-ascription from prakṛti and its products [[SK 64](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/64)]. When that knowledge has done its work and the body finally falls away, the result is kaivalya in the strict sense: both certain and final [[SK 68](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/68)].

In Yoga, liberation is kaivalya, the hāna that consists in the cessation of puruṣa-prakṛti saṃyoga once avidyā is absent [[YS 2.25](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.25)]. Its immediate upāya is aviplavā vivekakhyāti, an unbroken discriminative discernment rather than mere episodic insight [[YS 2.26](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.26)]. At the culmination, kaivalya is described as the pratiprasava of the guṇas once they are empty of further puruṣārtha, or equally as citiśakti abiding in its own svarūpa [[YS 4.34](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/4.34)].

Nyāya defines apavarga through the reverse cessation of the saṃsāric chain: when mithyājñāna is undone, doṣa, pravṛtti, janma, and duḥkha successively fall away [[NS 1.1.2](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.2)]. The state itself is atyantavimokṣa, total and non-recurrent release [[NS 1.1.22](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.22)]. Vaiśeṣika frames the same end from the side of causal ontology: when the conditions generating fresh conjunction are absent, there is non-manifestation, and that condition is mokṣa [[VS 5.2.20](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/5.2.20)].

*Mokṣa* is the exhaustion of *karma* and *kleśa*, and these in turn depend upon *vikalpa* and *prapañca*; when proliferative elaboration is stilled in *śūnyatā*, the whole bondage-series collapses [[MMK 18.5](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.5)]. That *śūnyatā* is not a separate absolute but dependent arising itself, the *madhyamā pratipad* articulated without *svabhāva*-reification [[MMK 24.18](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.18)]. Accordingly, *nirvāṇa* or *mokṣa* cannot be coherently stated as a positive entity, an absence, or a hybrid of the two [[MMK 25.11](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.11)].

In the Yogācāra materials available here, the functional equivalent of mokṣa is āśrayasya parāvṛtti, the turning-about of the basis that coincides with supramundane cognition after the abandonment of the twofold dauṣṭhulya [[Triṃśikā 29](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/29)]. That very transformation is the anāsrava dhātu, stable and wholesome, and as the Mahāmune's dharmakāya it is also called the vimuktikāya [[Triṃśikā 30](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/30)].

For the Pratyabhijñā, bondage is the condition of the pramātṛ blinded by māyā and bound by karma, whereas liberation is the recovery of aiśvarya through vidyā, the recognition of oneself as cidghana rather than as a contracted subject [[ĪPK 3.2.2](cite://utpaladeva/ishvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3.2.2)]. The liberated subject accordingly does not meet the common object as radically other, but apprehends it under svātmābheda, whereas the bound subject cognizes it under atyantabheda [[ĪPK 4.13](cite://utpaladeva/ishvara-pratyabhijna-karika/4.13)]. The path therefore culminates in continuous immersion in śivatā through the recognition of the self's own world-creating lordship [[ĪPK 4.16](cite://utpaladeva/ishvara-pratyabhijna-karika/4.16)].

Jainism defines the path to liberation through the triad of samyagdarśana, samyagjñāna, and samyakcāritra, and it places mokṣa within the basic ontology of jīva, ajīva, āsrava, bandha, saṃvara, nirjarā, and mokṣa [[TS 1.1](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.1); [TS 1.4](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.4)]. The operative discipline is the stoppage of karmic influx by saṃvara together with the shedding of accumulated karma by nirjarā [[TS 9.1](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/9.1); [TS 9.3](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/9.3)]. Mokṣa itself is the complete release from all karma through the absence of the causes of bondage together with nirjarā [[TS 10.2](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/10.2)].

per_school.not_applicable_reason: The Pāṇinian-Vaiyākaraṇa tradition's distinctive doctrinal apparatus is grammatical and semantic rather than a school-specific soteriology of mokṣa, so this term is not a native glossary target for that school.

translator_note: 'Liberation' is the least bad English gloss, but it easily imports a modern picture of escape, privacy, or merely negative freedom. *Mokṣa* is not one uniform postmortem reward, and it is not always an exit from the world as such. The Sanskrit term carries the metaphysical dispute inside it: whether what is removed is *avidyā*, karmic embodiment, distance from Bhagavān, or contracted self-recognition.
